Lessons from an interview: Join algorithms and why “just write one big SQL query” is often a bad idea.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI image“Our Architect Doesn’t Allow More Than Three JOINs”
During one of my interviews, the interviewer casually mentioned a rule that immediately caught my attention:
“In many projects, architects discourage SQL statements that join more than three tables.”
At first, it sounded like an arbitrary coding standard.
After all, SQL supports joining dozens of tables. Modern databases are incredibly powerful.
So why impose such a restriction?
The rest of the interview answered that question — and it turned into one of the best lessons I’ve ever learned about database performance.
Understanding SQL JOIN
JOIN combines rows from multiple tables.
Common JOIN types include:

• INNER JOIN

• LEFT JOIN

• RIGHT JOIN

• FULL OUTER JOIN
A simple example:
SELECT c.name,
o.order_id
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.id =…
